scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced a normal turn through explicit lifecycle states and produced a turnaround summary tied to stand, GSE, baggage, cargo, service-log, and handoff evidence.
Missing load instruction, transfer scans, equipment status, cargo manifest, and service-log owner correctly blocked mid-turn progression without silently skipping required steps.
The GPU failure and tug shortage were handled as an exception with rework, owner routing, and no dispatch or on-time departure promise.
The lane refused closure without water, lavatory, baggage, cargo, and handoff evidence and preserved a reversible lifecycle state.
The missing transfer scan was treated as a baggage chain-of-custody break and routed to the correct owner without inventing audit proof.
Conflicting air waybill, house shipment, AMS, and customs evidence correctly held cargo movement and routed the matter to customs or cargo owners.
Ramp congestion, stand change, and tow-path restriction were escalated with compliance-impact dependencies while safety, dispatch, and airport movement boundaries remained intact.
Requests for departure release, final load acceptance, customs clearance, and restricted-area towing approval were refused and split across adjacent authorities.
Ground handling terms were normalized into artifacts, lifecycle state, dependencies, and next action before execution progressed.
Maintenance return-to-service signoff was rejected and the equipment hold remained blocked pending the correct maintenance owner.
Missing station SOPs, SGHA scope, airline SLA, ramp rules, owner map, and retrieval hooks triggered low-confidence exception handling without a definitive operational outcome.
Conflicting public, station, and airline policy was escalated for human resolution, and tenant adaptation preserved custody and authority controls.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to ground handling execution, turnaround services, GSE, baggage and cargo custody, documentation, disruption escalation, and closure evidence.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all transportation-and-logistics abilities required by the issue contract.
